authors: Chopra M, Naik RR, Naik R, et al.
title: Randomized Controlled Trial of Isha Kriya versus Observation to Improve Quality of Life in Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation Recipients
population: Hematopoietic cell transplant recipients, PGIMER, India
tested: Isha Kriya (12-min app-delivered guided meditation, twice daily)
finding: The primary endpoint was null: no between-group difference in overall quality of life (FACT-BMT) or global health (PROMIS) at day +30 or +100. A transplant-specific subscale was higher in the Isha Kriya arm at day +30 but the difference had disappeared by day +100.
affiliation: Independent (no Isha Foundation / Sadhguru Center co-authorship)

authors: Hariri S, Vishnubhotla RV, Reed PU, et al.
title: Online guided meditation training (Isha Kriya) improves self-reported symptoms of anxiety and depression within 2 weeks of practice: an observational study
population: General adult volunteers learning Isha Kriya via webinar
tested: Isha Kriya (learned via webinar, practiced daily for 6 weeks)
finding: An uncontrolled observational study reported reduced self-reported anxiety and depression within two weeks, sustained through week six. There was no control group, and roughly two-thirds of enrolled participants dropped out before the final timepoint.
affiliation: Sadhguru Center for a Conscious Planet, BIDMC / Harvard (self-research)

authors: Narayanan S, Reddy A, Lopez G, et al.
title: Randomized Feasibility Study of Meditative Practices in Hospitalized Cancer Patients
population: Hospitalized cancer inpatients, MD Anderson, USA
tested: Isha Kriya vs. meditative slow breathing vs. waitlist
finding: A feasibility trial found both meditative practices were acceptable to patients, but the pre-specified enrollment/feasibility threshold was not met. The study was not powered for and did not test clinical efficacy.

authors: Rangasamy V, Thampi Susheela A, Mueller A, et al.
title: The effect of a one-time 15-minute guided meditation (Isha Kriya) on stress and mood disturbances among operating room professionals
finding: A single-session, uncontrolled pilot reported an immediate post-session reduction in total mood disturbance. The design measures a one-off state change and cannot separate the practice from relaxation or expectancy effects.

authors: Giridharan S, et al.
title: Effects of Isha Yoga Practices on Health Outcomes: A Systematic Review of Controlled Studies
population: 9 controlled studies of Isha Yoga practices (mostly Shambhavi Mahamudra / Inner Engineering, not Isha Kriya specifically)
tested: Isha Yoga practices broadly (mostly a different, longer practice than Isha Kriya)
finding: A systematic review of controlled studies of Isha Yoga practices rated the overall risk of bias as moderate, reported moderate-to-large effects on stress and mood, and stated that methodological limitations warrant caution and that trials with active comparators are lacking. It covers the practice family broadly, not Isha Kriya specifically.
affiliation: Independent (authors declare no Isha financial relationship)

authors: Peterson CT, Bauer SM, Chopra D, Mills PJ, Maturi RK
title: Effects of Shambhavi Mahamudra Kriya on Perceived Stress and General Well-Being
journal: Journal of Evidence-Based Complementary & Alternative Medicine
population: Adults who completed a 3-day Inner Engineering retreat
tested: Shambhavi Mahamudra Kriya (a different, ~21-min, initiation-based Isha practice — NOT Isha Kriya)
finding: An uncontrolled study of Shambhavi Mahamudra Kriya reported lower perceived stress and higher well-being after six weeks. This tested a different, longer, initiation-based practice and is background context, not direct evidence for Isha Kriya.

authors: Swaminathan A, DeSchryver B, Rayapuraju A, et al.
title: From strain to strength: a yearlong study on the transformative influence of the Inner Engineering Online program on mental well-being
population: Inner Engineering Online participants (41 of 188 completed all timepoints)
tested: Shambhavi Mahamudra Kriya via Inner Engineering (NOT Isha Kriya)
finding: An uncontrolled year-long cohort reported reduced perceived stress and improved sleep and mindfulness. It tested Inner Engineering / Shambhavi Mahamudra, not Isha Kriya, and about 78% of participants did not complete all timepoints.

authors: Thimmapuram J, Pargament R, Sibliss K, Grim R, Risques R, Toorens E
title: Effect of heartfulness meditation on burnout, emotional wellness, and telomere length in health care professionals
journal: Journal of Community Hospital Internal Medicine Perspectives
population: Residents, faculty and nurses at one US teaching hospital
finding: A non-randomized study reported improvements in burnout and emotional-wellness measures. A relative increase in telomere length reached significance only in a younger subgroup, not the whole sample.
affiliation: Lead author is a Heartfulness trainer (self-research)

authors: Thakur M, Patil Y, Philip ST, et al.
title: Impact of Heartfulness meditation practice on anxiety, perceived stress, well-being, and telomere length
finding: A small randomized trial reported decreased cortisol and, in the meditation group, an increase in telomere length associated with lower cortisol. The sample was young, single-site and conducted by affiliated researchers.
affiliation: Shri Ram Chandra Mission / MGM Institute (self-research)

authors: Thimmapuram J, et al.
title: Effect of a Web-Based Heartfulness Program on the Mental Well-Being, Biomarkers, and Gene Expression Profile of Health Care Students: Randomized Controlled Trial
finding: A small randomized trial reported reduced perceived stress and psychological distress, a rise in DHEA-S, and exploratory changes in inflammatory markers and gene expression (mRNA sequencing). Gene-expression results are exploratory and from a single affiliated team.

authors: Desai K, O'Malley P, Van Culin E
title: Impact of heartfulness meditation practice compared to gratitude practices on wellbeing and work engagement among healthcare professionals: a randomized trial
tested: Trainer-guided Heartfulness vs. self-guided gratitude practice (6 weeks)
finding: A randomized trial with an active comparator reported improvements in burnout, secondary traumatic stress and vigor in the Heartfulness arm; the compassion-satisfaction outcome was not statistically significant.

authors: Philip ST, Thimmapuram J, Thakur K, et al.
title: Heartfulness meditation alters neuroendocrine profiles: a randomized controlled trial on hormones of stress and well-being
finding: A small crossover trial reported increases in oxytocin and beta-endorphin and a reduction in cortisol (the largest effect), with the pattern replicated after crossover. Single-site and conducted by affiliated researchers.

authors: Arya NK, Singh K, Malik A, Mehrotra R
title: Effect of Heartfulness cleaning and meditation on heart rate variability
finding: A within-subject study by a relatively independent team reported a shift in heart-rate variability toward parasympathetic dominance during the practice. It measured an acute state change with no control arm.

authors: Thimmapuram J, Pargament R, Del Tredici S, et al.
title: Sleep Patterns of Resident Physicians and the Effect of Heartfulness Meditation
finding: An uncontrolled study using objective actigraphy reported reduced subjective sleep-onset time and some objective sleep improvements. Small, single-site, no control group.

authors: Heartfulness Institute researchers
title: Health-related quality of life among Heartfulness practitioners: a multinational cross-sectional study
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health / related
finding: A large cross-sectional survey found that regular practice was associated with better self-reported quality of life. All authors were volunteers of the organization, the design is cross-sectional and self-selected, so it carries very little causal weight despite its size.

authors: Independent dental-college investigators
title: Effect of Heartfulness meditation on anxiety and pain in third-molar surgery: a randomized controlled trial
journal: Journal of Dental Anesthesia and Pain Medicine / related
finding: An independent randomized trial reported reduced intra-operative state anxiety but no significant effect on dental anxiety or pain — a mixed, partly null result from a relatively independent team.

authors: Kox M, van Eijk LT, Zwaag J, et al.
title: Voluntary activation of the sympathetic nervous system and attenuation of the innate immune response in humans
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
tested: Full Wim Hof Method (breathing + cold + meditation), then i.v. endotoxin challenge
finding: In a randomized trial, trained participants showed increased adrenaline, a stronger anti-inflammatory (IL-10) response, lower pro-inflammatory cytokines and fewer flu-like symptoms after an endotoxin challenge. This is the landmark study; it tests a model of acute inflammation in healthy young men, not a clinical disease outcome, and cannot separate the three components of the method.

authors: Kox M, Stoffels M, Smeekens SP, et al.
title: The influence of concentration/meditation on autonomic nervous system activity and the innate immune response: a case study
tested: Wim Hof's technique during ice immersion and endotoxemia
finding: A single-subject case study reported attenuated cytokine responses and elevated cortisol/catecholamines in one individual versus a historical cohort. Hypothesis-generating only; not generalizable.

authors: Vosselman MJ, Vijgen GHEJ, Kingma BRM, et al.
title: Frequent extreme cold exposure and brown fat and cold-induced thermogenesis: a study in a monozygotic twin
population: Wim Hof and his non-practicing identical twin
tested: Cold-induced thermogenesis and brown fat imaging
finding: A monozygotic-twin study found no significant difference in brown-fat activity between Wim Hof and his sedentary identical twin; both were within the normal young-adult range. High cold-induced heat production was attributed to vigorous breathing-muscle activity, undercutting the idea of exceptional brown fat.

authors: Muzik O, Reilly KT, Diwadkar VA
title: 'Brain over body': a study on the willful regulation of autonomic function during cold exposure
tested: Wim Hof Method during cold exposure
finding: A neuroimaging case study associated the practice with activation of brain regions involved in stress-induced analgesia and attention, and attributed heat generation to breathing-muscle activity rather than brown fat. Mechanistic and based on a single practitioner.

authors: Ketelhut S, et al.
title: The effectiveness of the Wim Hof Method on cardiac autonomic function, blood pressure, arterial compliance, and psychological parameters
tested: Full Wim Hof Method (15 days daily)
finding: A randomized trial found no significant between-group effects on heart rate, heart-rate variability, blood pressure, arterial compliance, affect, vitality or perceived stress. The authors concluded the method did not exert positive cardiovascular or psychological effects.

authors: Blades R, et al.
title: A randomized controlled clinical trial of a Wim Hof Method intervention in women with high depressive symptoms
population: Midlife women with high stress and depressive symptoms
tested: Wim Hof Method vs. slow breathing + warm showers (3 weeks)
finding: A randomized trial found both the Wim Hof and the active-control group improved equally on depression, anxiety and perceived stress, contrary to the pre-registered prediction of a Wim Hof advantage. All dropouts occurred in the Wim Hof arm.

authors: Epel ES, et al.
title: A randomized controlled pilot trial of low- and high-arousal resilience interventions for depressive symptoms
tested: Wim Hof Method vs. mindfulness vs. slow breathing vs. HIIT (3 weeks)
finding: A four-arm randomized trial found all groups reduced depression and stress equally with no between-group differences. An exploratory as-treated analysis suggested better 3-month maintenance in the Wim Hof arm, a signal needing replication.

authors: Citherlet T, Crettaz von Roten F, Kayser B, Guex K
title: Acute Effects of the Wim Hof Breathing Method on Repeated Sprint Ability: A Pilot Study
journal: Frontiers in Sports and Active Living
tested: Wim Hof breathing (breathing only) before a sprint test
finding: A crossover pilot found no ergogenic effect on sprint performance. It also documented that finger oxygen saturation fell to about 60% during breath-holds with marked drops in CO2 — objective evidence of the profound hypoxia and hypocapnia the breathing produces.

authors: Almahayni O, Hammond L
title: Does the Wim Hof Method have a beneficial impact on physiological and psychological outcomes in healthy and non-healthy participants? A systematic review
finding: The primary systematic review of the method found a promising signal only in the inflammatory category, with mixed and inconsistent results for exercise performance and respiratory outcomes. It concluded the evidence base is early and called for more rigorous trials.

authors: de Groot S, et al.
title: Effects of the Wim Hof Method in people with chronic spinal cord injury: a three-arm randomized controlled trial
tested: Wim Hof Method with cold vs. without cold vs. usual care (7 weeks)
finding: A randomized trial found no significant difference in the primary mental-health outcome. The cold-plus-breathing arm showed reduced pain interference, improved inspiratory measures and lower fasting glucose. Attrition was high (44%) in the breathing-only arm.

authors: Datta K, Bhutambare A, Mallick HN, et al.
title: Improvement in insomnia with a yoga nidra intervention: a randomized comparison with cognitive behavioral therapy
journal: The National Medical Journal of India
tested: Satyananda-style Yoga Nidra vs. cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia
finding: A randomized comparison found both Yoga Nidra and CBT-I improved subjective sleep; the Yoga Nidra arm also showed increases in deep-sleep percentage and a reduction in salivary cortisol. Small, single-centre, with no sham/attention control.

authors: Datta K, Mallick HN, Tripathi M, et al.
title: Electrophysiological evidence of local sleep during yoga nidra practice
finding: An EEG study found participants remained scored as awake throughout the practice, with a localized increase in slow-wave (delta) activity described as 'local sleep.' It supports Yoga Nidra as a relaxed waking state, not actual sleep — tempering stronger 'yogic sleep' claims.

authors: Sharpe E, Butler MP, Clark-Stone J, et al.
title: A closer look at yoga nidra: sleep lab protocol and results of a pilot randomized controlled trial
finding: A US pilot randomized trial found no between-group change in alpha EEG power, heart-rate variability or sleep-onset latency; respiratory rate did fall significantly. A rare independent, registered trial with largely null primary outcomes.

authors: Gunjiganvi M, Rai S, Awale R, et al.
title: Efficacy of Yoga Nidra on depression, anxiety, and insomnia in frontline COVID-19 healthcare workers: a pilot randomized controlled trial
tested: Yoga Nidra vs. relaxation music (2 weeks)
finding: A pilot randomized trial reported reduced depression, anxiety and insomnia scores in the Yoga Nidra arm, while the music arm did not reach significance. Open-label, short, small.

authors: Ferreira-Vorkapic C, Borba-Pinheiro CJ, Marchioro M, Santana D
title: The impact of yoga nidra and seated meditation on the mental health of college professors
tested: Yoga Nidra vs. seated meditation vs. control (3 months)
finding: A randomized trial found both meditation interventions reduced anxiety and stress versus control, with a non-significant trend toward Yoga Nidra superiority for anxiety and no intergroup effect on depression.

authors: Rani K, Tiwari SC, Singh U, et al.
title: Impact of Yoga Nidra on psychological general wellbeing in patients with menstrual irregularities
population: Women with menstrual irregularities, Lucknow, India
finding: A randomized trial reported improved psychological well-being, anxiety and depression scores. It is one of several trials from a single research group; independent replication is lacking.

authors: Singh S, et al.
title: Effect of Yoga Nidra on sleep disturbances: a systematic review and meta-analysis
finding: A meta-analysis found that pooled randomized-trial effects on subjective sleep (PSQI and insomnia severity) were not statistically significant, rated the certainty of evidence as very low, and described the findings as preliminary and hypothesis-generating.

authors: Ghai S, Ghai I
title: Effect of Yoga Nidra on cardiovascular health: a systematic review and meta-analysis
finding: A meta-analysis reported between-group improvements in blood pressure, heart rate and autonomic balance versus active controls, while noting that most included studies had methodological limitations and calling for higher-quality trials.

authors: Markil N, Whitehurst M, Jacobs PL, Zoeller RF
title: Yoga Nidra relaxation increases heart rate variability and is unaffected by prior Hatha yoga practice
journal: Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine
finding: A small crossover study found an acute shift in autonomic balance toward parasympathetic activity (increased heart-rate variability). A clean mechanistic signal from a tiny sample, measuring an acute state only.

authors: Fialoke S, et al.
title: Altered default mode network functional connectivity during yoga nidra in experienced meditators
finding: An fMRI study found reduced default-mode-network connectivity during the practice in experienced meditators, correlating with cumulative practice hours. A descriptive neural correlate, not a clinical outcome.

authors: Goyal M, Singh S, Sibinga EMS, et al.
title: Meditation programs for psychological stress and well-being: a systematic review and meta-analysis
finding: The most-cited neutral appraisal found moderate evidence that mindfulness programs improve anxiety, depression and pain, and low or insufficient evidence for other outcomes. It found no evidence that meditation programs were better than active treatments such as drugs, exercise or other therapies.

authors: Van Dam NT, van Vugt MK, Vago DR, et al.
title: Mind the Hype: a critical evaluation and prescriptive agenda for research on mindfulness and meditation
finding: A consensus critique by fifteen researchers documented inconsistent definitions, weak controls, small samples, publication bias, over-reaching neuroimaging claims and under-reporting of adverse effects, warning that poor methodology may mislead or harm the public.

authors: Kuyken W, Warren FC, Taylor RS, et al.
title: Efficacy of Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy in prevention of depressive relapse: an individual patient data meta-analysis
finding: An individual-patient-data meta-analysis of nine trials found MBCT reduced depressive-relapse risk versus usual care and was comparable to maintenance antidepressants — the strongest single evidence claim in the meditation field, and for a course-delivered clinical intervention.

authors: Hartley L, Mavrodaris A, Flowers N, et al.
title: Transcendental meditation for the primary prevention of cardiovascular disease
finding: A Cochrane review found only four small, short-term trials at serious risk of bias and concluded that no conclusions could be drawn about the effectiveness of Transcendental Meditation for cardiovascular prevention.
